name: Microsoft Playwright
what_it_does: Playwright is an open-source browser automation library that lets agents control Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit browsers programmatically via code — not a REST API.
use_cases: Automated end-to-end testing of web applications from within agent workflows
Scraping JavaScript-rendered pages that require full browser execution
Taking screenshots or generating PDFs of web pages for visual analysis
Filling forms, clicking buttons, and interacting with web UIs on behalf of a user
Connecting to managed cloud browser services (Browserbase, Browserless) via CDP for scalable agent browsing
not_for: Teams needing a hosted/managed REST API — Playwright is a self-managed library requiring Node.js or Python runtime
Environments where installing npm/pip packages is not possible
Simple HTTP-based web scraping where a headless browser is overkill
best_when: Best when you need precise, scriptable browser control in a Node.js or Python environment and want broad cross-browser coverage including Firefox and WebKit.
avoid_when: Avoid when you need a managed cloud service with no infrastructure overhead — use Browserbase or Browserless instead.

auth.notes: No authentication required. Playwright is an open-source library installed via npm or pip. When connecting to cloud browser providers via CDP, authentication is handled at the provider level.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited — fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license
pricing.paid_tiers: None — the library itself is free. Cloud browser infrastructure providers (Browserbase, Browserless) that Playwright can connect to have their own pricing.
pricing.notes: Playwright itself has no cost. Operational costs come from the compute resources needed to run browsers locally or from third-party cloud browser services.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — constrained only by local machine resources or the cloud provider being used

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Playwright throws descriptive exceptions with selectors and timeout context, though async stack traces can be difficult to parse in agent error-handling loops.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Browser actions are inherently stateful and not idempotent; page state must be managed explicitly by the agent. Navigation can be re-issued but side effects (form submissions, clicks) are not idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Browser context and page objects are stateful — agents must manage lifecycle (open/close) carefully to avoid memory leaks and dangling sessions
Dynamic selectors from AI-generated code frequently break on page structure changes; use data-testid attributes or aria roles for resilience
Playwright requires a running browser binary; containerized agent environments must include Chromium/Firefox system dependencies or use a remote CDP endpoint
waitForSelector and waitForNavigation timeouts default to 30 seconds — agents that proceed on timeout errors can end up acting on incomplete page state
Cross-origin iframes require separate frame handles; agents using page.$() on the top-level document will silently miss content inside iframes

security_score_components.security_notes: As a library, security posture is largely determined by the consuming application. Browsers launched by Playwright have access to the full local filesystem and network unless sandboxed. Running with --no-sandbox (common in Docker) significantly reduces isolation.
